title: Colloidal oat cold-process soap
subtitle: A gentle, exfoliating bar for sensitive and dry skin
excerpt: A cold-process bar soap with finely milled colloidal oatmeal stirred in at trace for gentle exfoliation and skin-calming properties. The oil base is 55% olive, 30% coconut, 15% castor. Unscented. Allow 4-6 weeks to harden before use.
sourceNotes: Susan Miller Cavitch, The Soapmaker's Companion (1997): natural additives for texture and skin effect. Scientific literature on colloidal oatmeal (Avena sativa) as a skin-protective barrier agent (FDA-approved skin protectant). SoapCalc (accessed May 2026): SAP values for this oil blend.

content.text: Colloidal oatmeal is oats milled to a flour-fine powder. It is one of the few cosmetic add-ins with solid research behind it for protecting skin. The beta-glucans and other compounds in oats form a brief barrier on the skin during a wash. In a cold-process bar, the oatmeal stays inert and gives a soft, slightly gritty feel that lifts dead skin without scratching. The simplest effective recipe for dry or reactive skin.
The bar is left unscented, which makes it suitable for skin that reacts to essential oils. The oil base is a mild, conditioning blend. Adding oatmeal at
trace
thickens the batter quickly; work fast once it goes in.
Wear protective gloves and splash-rated goggles for lye work. Add lye to water in a ventilated space.
What you need

items.name: Castor oil (cosmetic)
items.prepNote: 15%. Higher castor for a creamy, stable lather that pairs well with the oatmeal texture.

items.name: Colloidal oatmeal
items.prepNote: 5% of oil weight. Cosmetic-grade, finely milled. Stir into a tablespoon of olive oil to make a slurry before adding to the batter at light trace. Not the same as porridge oats or oat flour.

content.text: Method
Prepare the oatmeal slurry
Mix the colloidal oatmeal with one tablespoon of the weighed olive oil, stirring until a smooth, paste-like slurry forms. Adding it as a slurry prevents dry clumps in the finished bar.
Make the lye solution and prepare the oils

Gloves and goggles on. Add the sodium hydroxide to the distilled water slowly and stir until dissolved. Allow to cool to 38-42°C. Melt the coconut oil, combine with the remaining olive oil and castor oil, and allow to reach 38-42°C.
Blend to trace and add oatmeal
Pour the lye solution into the oils. Stick-blend in short bursts to reach light
trace
. Add the oatmeal slurry and stir in by hand until evenly distributed. The
saponification
process continues while you stir; the oatmeal will thicken the batter quickly. Pour into the mould without delay.
Mould and cure
Insulate with light coverings and leave 24-48 hours. Wear gloves to unmould and cut. The oatmeal gives the cut face a speckled, warm appearance. Cure on an open rack for 4-6 weeks. The
superfat
oils and oatmeal work together in the finished bar: the oils soften the skin and the oatmeal calms any reactive response during washing.
Storage and shelf life

Keep cured bars on a draining rack in a cool, dark spot. The bar keeps 2 years from cure date. Colloidal oatmeal does not shorten shelf life. Discard on DOS (orange spots on the cut face) or a rancid smell.
